name: The Coca-Cola Company
business: Global non-alcoholic beverage maker selling sparkling soft drinks, water, juice, sports and coffee brands through a worldwide concentrate-and-bottling model.
drivers: Concentrate volume, pricing power, brand strength, and emerging-market consumption growth.
risks: Sugar and health regulation, shifting consumer preferences toward healthier drinks, and large foreign-exchange exposure.
peers: Competes most directly with PepsiCo in beverages, sharing distribution, commodity, and FX dynamics.

name: PepsiCo, Inc.
business: Food and beverage company combining a beverage portfolio with Frito-Lay and Quaker snacks, giving it more diversification than a pure beverage maker.
drivers: Snack volume growth, beverage pricing, and global distribution scale.
risks: Commodity input costs, health trends, and currency swings.
peers: Overlaps heavily with Coca-Cola in beverages; the two are a classic consumer-staples pair.

name: Visa Inc.
business: Operates the largest electronic payments network, earning fees on the number and value of transactions routed between banks, merchants, and consumers.
drivers: Consumer-spending volume, cross-border travel, and the secular shift from cash to cards.
risks: Interchange-fee regulation, recession-driven spending slowdowns, and new payment rails.
peers: Near-identical business model to Mastercard, making the two a textbook economic twin.

name: Mastercard Incorporated
business: Runs a global card-processing network collecting fees on transaction volume across credit, debit, and commercial cards.
drivers: The same consumer-spending and cross-border travel trends that drive Visa.
risks: Interchange regulation and spending cycles, mirroring Visa's risk profile.
peers: Moves closely with Visa; the pair shares nearly all fundamental drivers.

name: Exxon Mobil Corporation
business: Integrated oil and gas major spanning exploration, production, refining, and chemicals.
drivers: Crude oil and natural-gas prices and global refining margins.
risks: Commodity-price volatility, the energy transition, and large capital-project execution.
peers: Shares crude-price exposure with Chevron, making the supermajors a natural pair.

name: Chevron Corporation
business: Integrated oil and gas company with upstream production and downstream refining.
drivers: The same crude-oil and natural-gas price cycles as other supermajors.
peers: Tracks ExxonMobil closely due to shared commodity exposure.

name: JPMorgan Chase & Co.
business: Largest U.S. bank, spanning consumer banking, corporate and investment banking, and asset management.
drivers: Net interest income, loan demand, credit quality, and capital-markets activity.
risks: Credit losses, regulation, and interest-rate moves.
peers: Shares the rate and credit cycle with Bank of America and other money-center banks.

name: Bank of America Corporation
business: Large diversified U.S. bank with consumer, commercial, and investment-banking arms.
drivers: Interest rates, loan growth, and credit conditions.
risks: The rate cycle, credit quality, and regulation.
peers: Strong economic overlap with JPMorgan; a common banking pair.

name: Pfizer Inc.
business: Global pharmaceutical company developing and selling branded medicines and vaccines.
drivers: Drug pipeline, patent exclusivity, and regulatory approvals.
risks: Patent cliffs, clinical-trial failures, and drug-pricing policy.
peers: Shares pipeline and regulatory dynamics with Merck.

name: Merck & Co., Inc.
business: Large pharmaceutical company focused on branded prescription drugs, oncology, and vaccines.
drivers: Pipeline strength, patent cycle, and vaccine demand.
risks: Patent expiry, pipeline setbacks, and pricing regulation.

name: Ford Motor Company
business: Designs and manufactures cars, trucks, and EVs, with a large financing arm.
drivers: Consumer demand, financing rates, input costs, and the EV transition.
risks: Economic cycles, EV-investment burn, and supply-chain disruption.
peers: Cyclical dynamics shared with General Motors.

name: General Motors Company
business: Legacy U.S. automaker producing trucks, SUVs, and EVs alongside a financing unit.
drivers: Vehicle demand cycles, interest-rate sensitivity, and EV-transition costs.

name: Apple Inc.
business: Designs consumer hardware (iPhone, Mac, iPad, Watch) plus a high-margin Services segment.
drivers: Device upgrade cycles and a growing installed base feeding Services.
risks: Hardware-demand cyclicality, supply-chain concentration, and App Store regulation.
peers: Large-cap tech, loosely related to Microsoft but with a hardware tilt.

name: Microsoft Corporation
business: Sells software, the Azure cloud platform, productivity tools, and gaming.
drivers: Recurring cloud and subscription revenue and enterprise demand.
risks: Cloud-growth deceleration, competition, and heavy AI-infrastructure spending.
peers: Mega-cap tech; shares macro sensitivity with Apple.
